Automated pattern recognition (which developers and media erroneously label "AI" for marketing purposes to excite the public) is a tool that is best used to do exactly what it says: quickly search for and correlate patterns in any set of data which would take a long time for humans to manually review. That is the technology's entire purpose, even when serving as a "chatbot" - it is not "thinking" of what to say, it is calculating probabilities of any given word following any other word based on predictable human speech patterns, converting words into numbers and adding them together to produce a mathematical outcome which is then converted back into words. This means in a chatbot function, APR can produce a wide range of "likely potential outcomes" to any query, taking unknown variables into account (just like trying to solve for X in an equation), and is very effective as a brainstorming and learning aid because of its ability to quickly expose users to a mass of information while requiring less search work and probably a lot less reading (since info can be condensed, compared to web articles where the tiny bit of info you want is hidden in a mountain of irrelevant paragraphs, and you have to dig through twenty links to get there). The downside is that the superficial similarity of mathematical pattern-recognition to actual reason causes users to become too trusting in a system which cannot differentiate fact and fiction and which has no specific ethical boundaries - like any other software program, it does what its programmer codes it to do, and nothing else. It doesn't care, it doesn't reason, and because of the fundamental limitations of computers and their programming this can't ever change (advocates of "general intelligence" are just bucking for more funding when they say otherwise - hence the imminent bursting of the "AI bubble" when people realize the investments do not correspond to actual advances or profitability). So, a bot is only useful for exploring "potential" ideas or patterns - a mirror reflecting the collective thoughts and behaviors of humans back to us in light of different data sets, serving somewhat like a  sounding board - not for telling you facts or trying to reason on its own. And for any situation which has moral, ethical, legal, or social/political implications, chatbots are completely useless except as social manipulation tools (repeating the arguments or highlighting the sources which human programmers tell the bot to prioritize). Whenever someone calls out this behavior, developers say "it is just the algorithm" but obviously any thinking person knows who wrote the algorithm, and they didn't write it by accident. Using bots as a social "influencer" (even for "benevolent purposes") will cause catastrophic harm to humans in the future, because it teaches humans to avoid thinking for themselves and to subjugate their moral reason to the control of amoral or even immoral entities. Whoever controls the bots controls "right" in that world, and can justify whatever evil they want to justify. That is also one reason why I think calling it "AI" instead of "APR" is a very bad idea, because AI erroneously tries to insinuate that the bot and its words can achieve some kind of equal footing with humans, while APR acknowledges that the bot is only an inanimate tool and should never be trusted any more than you would trust a screwdriver. Tools are tools, not friends, not "romantic partners", not religious leaders or lifestyle gurus. Tools are just tools. Any "AI" company that tries to develop this tech for any other purpose than to serve as a tool should be shunned by investors and regulators.

In my personal life, I use ai for different purposes. For example, chatbots like ChatGPT is something I've been using to summarize long articles that I don't feel like fully reading. Just in the past few days, I've also been  using it to help me shop on Amazon for new computer cables and even curtain rods. I have to say that it doesn't fully follow instructions, because when I told it to limit the results to Amazon, it still kept giving me results from Home Depot and others. As for other uses, I use Suno to generate music that I use for my action video hobby. I just type what genre I want the song to be in, what voice, what the song's about, and then after around 3 minutes it outputs 2 songs for me to listen to, which is quite amazing. As for what I won't rely on ai for, it's very important stuff, like medical things and statistics. Actually, I also can't fully trust it when it comes to shopping. For example, the other day I was asking it on how the ebay auction worked. It gave me a whole description and even instructions and tips on how to win an auction I was interested in. When I followed what ChatGPT said, ebay actually rejected my attempt and it showed me that they have a different way of bidding on things. When I confronted ChatGPT about this bad info, it said that it based the instructions it gave me on outdated info. I asked it why it didn't check for the most recent way ebay auctions actually worked, and it just apologized and at one point, it even became confrontational and put words in my mouth, which I then pointed out as being false as it was already hallucinating. I made it review our conversation and once again, it apologized for becoming overly defensive to the point that it falsified information in order to make it seem it was my fault. This is why I can never fully trust ai.
